title: add "name" function to pdgid/functions.py
body: There is no function in pdgid/functions.py which returns the name of the particle with a given PDGID. I think there should be such a function and I would also like the `__repr__` of PDGID show this name. The name should be generated from the tables in data.

title: "-" or "~" should turn a PDGID object from particle to its anti-particle
body:
I think the operator logic for the PDGID itself should also apply to its wrapper, the PDGID class. The "-" operator should be defined to turn particles into anti-particles. e.g. -pi_plus == pi_minus. Before jumping ahead and implementing this, a decision has to be made, because the "~" operator could also be used in this way.

* I personally prefer operator "\~", because it conveys the concept of "anti" better. When people write about anti particles in ASCII, I have seen the notation with "~", but never with "-".

* For people who are already used to the logic of raw PDGIDs, using operator "-" is more obvious than "~".

I could live with both decisions.

title: Particle ID translations among generators
body:
The module [particletools](https://github.com/afedynitch/ParticleDataTool) from Anatoli Fedynitch has a strong overlap with `particle`. I hope he will join forces with us to develop this module further, merging the capabilities of `particletools` in `particle`.

One thing that `particletools` has is the ability to translate from/to PDGID to various other ID systems which are out there. For example, CORSIKA (the leading cosmic ray air shower simulation code in cosmic ray physics) has its own system, Pythia has its own system, EPOS (popular hadronic interaction model) has its own system, ...

It would be nice to add a `foreign_ids` system, which can translate from/to PDGID. The translation tables should be included in the module for convenience, and preferably in a way that makes it easy for users to provide additional tables.

Technically, you would need what is called a bimap in Boost, a map that allows fast lookup from both key and value. See:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1456373/two-way-reverse-map
http://paddy3118.blogspot.com/2009/04/bimap-bi-directional-mappingdictionary.html
There is currently no standard lib implementation of a bimap as far as I know. Using just a dict as suggested in some answers of SO, does not avoid collisions. Collision example: in system A gamma == 1, in system B u == 1. What should dict[1] return, gamma or u? The way out is to use different enum classes as keys. So in addition to PDGID, there could be PYTHIAID and EPOSID and CORSIKAID. Then the lookup with a normal dict would work, because the keys while having colliding numerical values, are now distinct types.

title: Issue with Particle.describe(...)
body:
Hi @henryiii,
There is a little issue with the parsing of particle info as seen by the first-line spurious characters in the examples below.

Python 3:

```
In [29]: from particle import Particle
In [30]: p=Particle.from_pdgid(5122)
In [31]: print(p.describe())
.2e .0e
Name: Lambda(b)  ID: 5122         Fullname: Lambda(b)0     Latex: $\Lambda_b^{0}$
Mass  = 5619.60 ± 0.17 MeV
Width = 4.48e-10 ± 3e-12 MeV
I (isospin)       = 0      G (parity)        = 0      Q (charge)       = 0
J (total angular) = 0.5    C (charge parity) = 0      P (space parity) = +
    Quarks: udb

```

Python 2:

```
In [3]: from particle import Particle
In [4]: p=Particle.from_pdgid(5122)
In [5]: print p.describe()

Mass  = 5619.60 +/- 0.17 MeV
Width = 4.48e-10 +/- 3e-12 MeV
```

title: improve from_search_list for real-valued particle properties and improve documentation
body:
`Particle.from_search_list` is better documented in the README than in the pydoc. In the README it says I can select particles by keywords, name, charge, status, etc. The system works fine for name, charge, and a few other discrete properties, but doesn't work well for masses. It is also not documented which argument type is expected in the docs.

For real-valued properties like the mass and life-time, I would like to be able to write something like this
```
from particle.search_variables import mass
Particle.from_search_list(mass > 100*MeV)
```
`mass` here is an instance of a new class SearchVariable, which has comparison operators defined in a special way that stores the comparison argument and the type of comparison and then acts as an unary predicate. Inside the `from_search_list` function, one would do (not actual code):
```
accept = []
for particle in table:
   if mass(particle.mass):
     accept.append(particle)
```

title: add life-time to particle properties
body: I cannot look up the life-time of particles, the information is missing. Life-time would very useful in LHCb and elsewhere for the definition of final state particles. A final state particle is one with a given minimum lifetime.

title: Allow lookup for decays and branching ratios
body:
`particletools` from Anatoli allows the user to look up particle decays and their branching ratios, which is very nice. Implementing this would require a different data source, a hierarchical data base. The current tabular format of the mcd and csv data tables cannot represent a variable number of branching ratios per particle.

`particletools` uses XML for that purpose, which works, but I would prefer yaml if the data base should be human readable. Another alternative is to code the data base directly in form of Python primitives, using dicts and list literals. It can be easily generated by a script and then "serialized" into a literal using `repr`.

title: Add tests for DecayLanguage particles
body:
The particles used by DecayLauage should be tested. This would require two tests:

- [x] One that reads in AmpGen style names and makes sure they all convert correctly to Particles.
- [x] One that reads in DecFile style names and makes sure they all convert correctly to Particles.

In both cases, the initial input to the mark.parameterize should be a dump from DecayLanguage of all names detected.

title: Better fullnames
body:
I think `Particle.fullname` should return better names. Some examples.

| name | fullname | expected fullname |
|------| ----------| -------------------|
|gamma | gamma0| gamma |
|p | p+ | p |
|u| u~+2/3| u~ |
|W| W-|  W-|

title: Enhance Particle documentation
body: `help(particle.Particle)` shows nice documentation for the methods, but docs are missing for many properties. Examples: C, G, I, P, anti, charge (say in which unit), latex, mass*, name, pdgid, quarks, rank, status, width*.

title: Add neutrinos to the package extension of the PDG extended file
body: This way neutrinos can be handled too, which will be relevant when using the package in decaylanguage's decay files.

title: Particle.radius property
body: It is not clear what this "obscure" property is for, nor what it returns. Documentation needs to be added, providing information on what this PDG data is, and a a reference to details in the PDG.

title: Add tests for Particle.html_name
body: Not a priority but worth writing down that some basic tests should be added.

title: Better handling of Particle.empty()
body: The method Particle.empty() is rather special given that, strictly speaking the PDGID=0 defined in it is not a valid ID. The method may be handy, though, so better have a handling of this "special particle" in an appropriate way. In other words, there should be no exceptions thrown when calling the class properties. This is not quite yet the case, hence this issue.

title: Suggestion to change the way particles are printed out
body:
It seems more natural to have
`<Particle: name='Lambda(b)0', pdgid=5122, mass=5619.60 ± 0.17 MeV>`
instead of
`<Particle: pdgid=5122, name='Lambda(b)0', mass=5619.60 ± 0.17 MeV>`

Likewise, the `.describe()` method presently returns
```
PDG name: K          ID: 311          Name: K0             Latex: $K^{0}$
Mass  = 497.611 ± 0.013 MeV
Width = -1.0 MeV
I (isospin)       = 1/2    G (parity)        = 0      Q (charge)       = 0
J (total angular) = 0.0    C (charge parity) = 0      P (space parity) = ?
    Quarks: dS
    Antiparticle status: Full (antiparticle name: K~0)
```

whereas the following seems more nicely organised:
```
Name: K0             ID: 311          Latex: $K^{0}$
Mass  = 497.611 ± 0.013 MeV
Width = -1.0 MeV
Q (charge)       = 0       J (total angular) = 0.0    P (space parity) = ?
C (charge parity) = 0      I (isospin)       = 1/2    G (parity)        = 0
    Quarks: dS
    Antiparticle name: K~0 (antiparticle status: Full)
```
In particular, the internal PDG table name `pdgname` is not printed.
Also the first line provides the most important and relevant quantum numbers. Indeed, C, I and G are not definable for many particles.

Agreed?

body:
Suggest to remove the tests of Enums (`particle.enums` submodule) from `test_particle.py` to a new file `test_enums.py`.

This is pure organisation. Nothing else.

- Add functionality to query which table(s) is(are) loaded.

Add a template file for how to create, and use, user-defined particles.
This is basically what is already being done in the [DecayLanguage](https://github.com/scikit-hep/decaylanguage/) package.

title: Should we do some doctest tests?
body: There's a fair amount of examples in the code and it would be ideal to make sure that those examples stay valid at all times. This can often be achieved with doctest tests (some examples may not be full, e.g. because of complex inputs, and those can be skipped).

title: Bug in the extended PDG file
body:
The extended PDG file, which we use to provide more particle info than that provided in the standard downloadable .mcd files, specify for example how to get the antiparticle name from the particle. There are 3 possibilities: self-conjugate (gamma, pi0, eta), same name but change of charge ( e- <--> e+, pi+ <--> pi-, K+ <--> K-, W+ <--> W- ), or change of charge sign with a bar (nu(e) <--> nubar(e), p <--> pbar, Delta++ <--> Deltabar--).

It turns out that all excited charged B mesons have been described in the 3rd category whereas they fall under the 2nd one, just as B+, D+, Ds+, Bc+. So one is getting for example

> B*+ <--> B*~-

whereas the correct "relation" is

> B*+ <--> B*-

I suggest we correct these in our extension to the extended file, just as we did for other issues, see the comments at the top of that file. I will submit a PR soon.

title: version.py to _version.py
body: Currently the version.py shows up in the tab completion list for particle; it should really be hidden, as most users should only access `__version__`.

title: SPAN breaks dot graphs
body: The addition of SPAN to the html_name breaks dot graphs. This should be optional, adjustable, or dot-friendly. Also not sure why this is uppercase.

title: Add CONTRIBUTING.md/.rst
body: @henryiii @eduardo-rodrigues It would be great to have a `CONTRIBUTING` guidelines file for this project. They're quite useful for having people from outside the core developers make useful contributions that aren't a pain for the dev team to review.

title: Documentation using Sphinx
body: Will the project support sphinx-based code documentation and adding RST manuals which will be compiled by sphinx into HTML/PDF based documentation?

title: Add a Particle.dump_table() method
body:
This should provide a nice printout of the whole CSV data table loaded.

Optionally, one could add an argument to save the table.

title: Documentation for certain manipulations, for reference
body:
It would be good to document somewhere how:

1) To obtain a list of all PDG IDs stored in the data file loaded.
`[p.pdgid for p in Particle.all()]`  for a list of `PDGID` instances.
`[int(p) for p in Particle.all()]`  for a list of PDG IDs, as ints.
2) To get the number of particles stored in the data file loaded.
`print(len(Particle.all()))`

title: Better dealing of undefined/non-measured particle widths
body:
Several known particles have no measured value of their width, e.g. the D∗(2007)0, with a width < 2.1 MeV @ 90% confidence level (see PDGLive). The present code shows
```
>>> p = Particle.from_pdgid(423)
>>> print(p.width, p.width_lower, p.width_upper)
2.1 1.0 -1.0
```
, which is "digested information" from the PDG particle data table: the .mcd file contains empty fields for the 3 quantities whereas the 2018 extended file flags the experimental upper limit with the values `2.1,-1,-1` (the fact the width_lower is +1 in the print out above is because all errors are converted to positive numbers in our converter code that produces the CSV file we use).    

We need to improve the above on 2 fronts:

1) `print(p.width, p.width_lower, p.width_upper)` should rather return `2.1 -1.0 -1.0` for this kind of cases, so that the user can see the errors are not known. The documentation could and should mention this special case.
Some code such as `df.loc[df['WidthLower'] != -1, 'WidthLower'] = 1` could do the trick.

2) With the special `-1.0` values for the width errors the method `Particle.describe()`  could catch these and print the particle width as `Width < 2.1 MeV` whereas now it prints `Width = 2.1 ± 1.0 MeV`, which is wrong and misleading.

One should also look at the mass and mass error entries to catch similar issues.

title: Particles only in so-called extended file should not be in the CSV
body:
I noticed that some non-established particles such as the K(4)(2500)+ are present in the 2008 extended file but aren't in the PDG .mcd files. At present these particles make it to our CSV files, so are available to the user. I reckon it is preferable to not have them in the CSV files.
Agreed, @henryiii?

Deal with this once https://github.com/scikit-hep/particle/pull/137 is merged.

body:
A call to `from_pdgid` with an unavailable value, results in the following stack trace

```python
>>> from particle import Particle
>>> Particle.from_pdgid(600095)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:\Projects\particle\particle\particle\particle.py", line 497, in from_pdgid
    return table[table.index(value)]
ValueError: 600095 is not in list

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "D:\Projects\particle\particle\particle\particle.py", line 499, in from_pdgid
    raise ParticleNotFound('Could not find PDGID {0}'.format(value))
particle.particle.particle.ParticleNotFound: Could not find PDGID 600095
```

Probably the error could be handled before the table lookup. The stack trace would be cleaner.

title: Black formatting
body: I think it would be a good idea to [format the code with Black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/). Would other devs (@eduardo-rodrigues) be fine with that?

title: custom field for mass shape
body:
As discussed with @eduardo-rodrigues , a custom field on a particle would allow to put attributes like a mass shape (for particles with a non-fixed mass mostly). This allows to connect the package with e.g. `phasespace`.

Or any alternative idea how to do it?

title: Consistency of Particle.width and Particle.lifetime
body:
As reported by @daritter, there is an inconsistency in the return values / exceptions in `Particle.width` and `Particle.lifetime`. This is due to the fact that the PDG does not always provide width values for all particles, or else it provides -1. How to make things consistent?

Specifically, "there are particles which currently throw a ValueError when accessing lifetime since the width is <0. I'm fine if this is intended. Yes, if we don't know the width we don't have a lifetime but then it would be maybe more consistent to also throw when width<0 as this doesn't make any physical sense either. Or just return NaN in both cases ...".

Opinions @henryiii, @mayou36?

title: Data files licenses?
body: We'd like to create a Julia version of this wonderful package and wanted to ask if we could reuse your `data` files. With proper citation of course!

title: Isospin for photons?
body:
The isospin `I` of photons is returning `<2`. It's bit confusing since `isospin` is related to strong interactions and W for example has no entry in the raw CSV file.

The `<2` seems to refer to a "weak isospin" which has an unfortunate naming and the same symbol `I`. Wondering how this could be cleaned up a little bit? For the Julia implementation we try to fix the types and the photon is the only exception.

I guess you also had a discussion before you decided to put a `<2` into the CSV `I` column? :wink: 

```python
>>> from particle import Particle
>>> g = Particle.from_pdgid(22)
>>> g.I
'<2'
```

title: Redundancy and inconsistency between setup.py and requirements.txt
body:
In setup.py, we have:
```
INSTALL_REQUIRES = [
    'enum34>=1.1; python_version<"3.4"',
    'importlib_resources>=1.0; python_version<"3.7"',
    "attrs>=17.4.0",
    "hepunits>=1.1.0",
]
```

In requirements.txt, we have:
```
enum34>=1.1; python_version<"3.4"
importlib_resources>=1.0; python_version<"3.7"
attrs
hepunits
tabulate
```

I propose to read the requirements in setup.py from requirements.txt to enforce consistency between them.

title: Design issue: tight tool coupling
body:
I looked into the `Particle.dump_table` method and saw some worrisome design choices, and so I wanted to write this down. To make it brief, adding convenience features is tempting, but it leads to interface and implementation bloat and one should really think twice about it. As a general rule, instead of calling some external tool inside your own interface, one should instead prepare the data in ones own tool so that the other external tool can easily consume it. This leads to a decoupling between Python packages, more combinatorical options for the user, and reduces interface, implementation, and unittest bloat.

This is a bit abstract, so let me point out what I mean specifically.

`dump_table` calles `tabulate` internally to make nice tables of the Particle data. This is nice, yes, but the same effect could have been obtained by just preparing the table data as a dict in Particle, and let the user pass this data to `tabulate`
```
tabulate(Particle.dump_table2(<options not related to tabulate>), <tabulate options>)
```
It is a really good idea to use `tabulate`, and this package can be recommended in the documentation, shown in examples, tutorials, mentioned in the FAQ. The call is an oneliner, there is barely more to type here than before.

But as the maintainer of Particle you save yourself a lot of work:
*You don't have to add, forward, document, and unittest keywords that change the output of `tabulate`, because the user is calling `tabulate` now and they can look up the options for `tabulate` themselves.*

If there is ever a package `super_tabulate` that is suddenly all hip with the kids, you don't have to do anything, users can just use that instead of `tabulate` (assuming that `super_tabulate` accepts the same input).

For me one of the key ideas of Scikit-HEP as a modular collection of specialized tools is to reduce direct coupling of tools like in `dump_table`, but offer generic and clear interfaces to pass data from these tools. This gives you enormous combinatorial power, and it reduces the implementation, documentation, and unittest load of each individual tool.

The fact that `dump_table` also supports a `filename` keyword to write to a file is just another variant of the same problem. Writing strings to a file is really easy in Python, you don't have to offer a functionality for that. Yes, it allows to quickly dump the output to a file, but it does not scale. What if I want to write it into a pipe instead of a normal file? What if I want to append the table to a file that has already some content? There are many ways to customize the action "write to a file-like object". If you try to support all those options, you need to add so many options to your interface. Or you just don't offer this shortcut. In Python there should be one way to do it. Writing stuff to files means you do `with open(filename, "w") as f: f.write(stuff)`. It is simple for simple things and scales to really complex things. And in Particle you only have to provide `stuff`.

```
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 174, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", fname, loader, pkg_name)
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 72, in _run_code
    exec code in run_globals
  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/__main__.py", line 3, in <module>
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 182, in run_module
    mod_name, loader, code, fname = _get_module_details(mod_name)
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 111, in _get_module_details
    __import__(mod_name)  # Do not catch exceptions initializing package
  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/particle/__init__.py", line 13, in <module>
  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/particle/geant/__init__.py", line 8, in <module>
  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/particle/geant/geant3id.py", line 19, in <module>
  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/particle/data/__init__.py", line 9, in <module>

  File "/Users/henryschreiner/Downloads/particle.pyz/importlib_resources/_compat.py", line 8, in <module>
ImportError: No module named pathlib2
```

I think this last issue is one that came up with newer versions of `importlib_resources` - we need to install pathlib2 now in the zipapp. The Python3 error seems to have come from a recent change where the datafile is being treated as an actual file - there is no promise in Python that files really will exist as a text file on a disk - when you are in a zip file, this is an example of a case where they are not real files.

title: Laziness feature request: `from particle import literals`
body:
Literals are great and I want to use them a lot!

Currently, I need to import literals like this
`from particle.particle import literals as lp`
For the end user it is not obvious why one has to type the middle `particle`, so this is a bit confusing (I think I understand why you organised it like this, but just saying).

It would be a great convenience if I could just import my literals like this
`from particle import literals as lp`

title: Test the notebooks
body: One could make use of the `papermill` package to extend the tests to the notebooks available. At present there is no 100% guarantee that developments don't break the notebooks.

title: Unexpected behavior of Particle.from_string
body:
When creating a proton from the string "p+", `Particle.from_string` returns a pion instead of a proton.
Things work fine when creating the particle from the string "p". 

**Reproducer**:
```
from particle import Particle
proton = Particle.from_string ("p+")
print (proton.name)  ## output: "pi+"
```

**Acceptable solution**:
The ability of Particle of trying to match particles by name without exact match is great, but when nested into other application wrong matches may result in tedious bugs difficult to debug. 
I would suggest to add an optional boolean flag to the "from_string" to allow raising an error in case of an imperfect match.

title: Import time
body: I've noticed that something that changed recently has caused the import time to skyrocket. (This could even just be in my branch). Running this in Python 3.7 with `python -X importtime` shows a whopping 10+ seconds devoted to `particle.particle.literals`!

title: Bug report: both baryons and antibaryons have parity +1
body:
Hello scikit-heppers,
Thanks for creating this nice package! It provides a useful interface to the PDG.

We are considering to use this package in the [`expertsystem`](https://pypi.org/project/expertsystem) package, but noticed a potential bug while trying to implement it (https://github.com/ComPWA/expertsystem/pull/190): it seems that all baryons and antibaryons have parity +1.

For example, in the following, both have `P (space parity) = +`:

```bash
python3 -m particle search 2212  # proton
python3 -m particle search -2212  # anti-proton
```

For a list of anti-baryons that (I guess) should have parity -1, see:

```python
from particle import Particle

Particle.findall(lambda p: p.pdgid.is_baryon and p.pdgid < 0 and p.P == +1)
```

This seems to be the case in both the [latest release](https://github.com/scikit-hep/particle/releases/tag/v0.10.0) and the latest commit cae76410d6c45e8c2df4982ae6edb6735c544b41.

title: Discussion on new PDGID qualifiers such as is_quarkonium
body:
Hi @HDembinski and @henryiii. I'm working towards a version 1.0 of the package, dealing with outstanding issues and committing various things I've had in the pipeline.

While producing for inspection a series of tables per particle "category" I realised that it might be neat to make the several
"helpers" (I had to write) readily available to the user - OK, some are trivial, but not all. So I would enhance the `PDGID` class with several boolean functions beyond what we already have, such as `is_lepton` or `is_diquark`. This is the kind of list I'm thinking about:

```
- is_sm_quark
- is_sm_lepton
- is_gauge_boson_or_higgs
- is_special_particle
- is_heavy_flavor
- is_light_meson = is_meson and not is_heavy_flavor
- is_strange_meson
- is_charmed_meson
- is_charmed_strange_meson
- is_bottom_meson
- is_bottom_strange_meson
- is_bottom_charmed_meson
- is_quarkonium or is_heavy_qqbar_meson
- is_light_baryon
- is_strange_baryon
- is_charmed_baryon
- is_bottom_baryon
```

It's basically giving the class a means to straightforwardly provide the classification the PDG uses in their MC numbering scheme document  https://pdg.lbl.gov/2020/reviews/rpp2020-rev-monte-carlo-numbering.pdf. This can be used as usual in searches with `Particle.findall(...)`. Adding this ensures the user does not have to implement it itself and may also prevent silly bugs ...

What do you think? Thanks.

title: Querying quark content from Particle is difficult, is there an easier way?
body:
Today I wanted to get all particles which have at least one strange quark. I found `Particle.quarks` and so I naively did:

```py
Particle.findall(lambda p: "s" in p.quarks.lower())
```
However, this finds also
```
pi0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
rho(770)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
a(2)(1320)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
rho(3)(1690)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
a(4)(1970)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
a(0)(1450)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
b(1)(1235)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
pi(2)(1670)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
a(1)(1260)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
rho(1700)0 (uU-dD)/sqrt(2)
[...]
```
It makes sense to me that `Particle.quarks` returns this, but it makes selecting particles by quark content surprisingly difficult. I would have run into the same issue if I asked for particles with a top quark.

I can work around this, no problem, but I wonder if there is an easier way to select particles by quark content, this seems like a common query.

1. Is there a better way to select by quark content?
2. Wouldn't it make sense to add further attributes to `Particle` to query strangeness, charmness, bottomness, etc?

title: Replace \bar with \overline and \pi with π etc
body:
The LaTeX strings for the particles are very nice and very useful, but they could be improved.
```py
from particle import literals as lp
lp.pi_plus.latex_name # '\\pi^{+}'
lp.Lambda_bar.latex_name # '\\bar{\\Lambda}'
```

1) Use upright greek letters. It is CERN wide style to use upright greek letters instead of slanted. The LHCb style imports the `upgreek` package for this, which we cannot do here, but we can replace `\\pi^{+}` with `π^{+}` to get the same effect. Likewise for other particles.

2) Use `\overline` instead of `\bar`. The LHCb style also uses `\overline` instead of `\bar`, since the latter does not look very good on wide characters like the Λ.

title: Add version of Particle.findall which returns generator?
body:
I love the API of `Particle.findall`. A minor nitpick for me is that it returns a list, while the modern way would be to return a generator.

I think it would be easy to change the implementation to return a generator. However, simply replacing the return type in this method with a generator would be a breaking change. So perhaps another method could be added that returns the generator, and `Particle.findall` internally just calls that. I am not sure how this should be called, but perhaps `Particle.finditer`?

title: Suggestion to deprecate and later remove `Particle.find`
body:
Let me start again with my nitpick by saying that I like `particle` very much, I use all the time in my code.

I suggest to deprecate `Particle.find` and to remove it in a future version. It is fully superseded by `Particle.findall` which is the correct interface for a query (well, actually #312 is). `Particle.find` can only return one result, but that is a very special case when the query fundamentally can yield none or several entries. Having such a restricted return type is not a good design and should be a non-starter. The API works around this by raising exceptions whenever the API contract cannot be fulfilled, this works correctly, but it's duck-tape over the more fundamental issue.

Scott Meyers says: "Make interfaces easy to use correctly and hard (or impossible) to use incorrectly". `Particle.find` is very difficult to use correctly (in the sense that you do not get an exception), because a priori, the user cannot know whether the query will yield exactly one result. Frequently, the results will contain zero or more entries, but these states cannot be represented by the method.

If you are not convinced yet, there are three other closely-related design principles which `Particle.find` violates. 

1) Each function/method should do the absolute minimum amount of work that is necessary to respond to the user request.

`find` always first finds all the matches and stores them in a temporary list. But it could stop already when the query found two matches, because then the result is clear, an exception has to be thrown. Also storing everything in a list only to discard that list afterwards is wasting resources. The implemention only needs to store the first match and once a second mathc is found, it can abort by raising an exception. This could be fixed by implementing `finditer` as suggested in #312 and use that internally instead of `findall`, but it would be better to just remove this method.

2) Whenever feasible, fully return the result of some work. In other words, minimize the amount of temporary data that is generated and then thrown away internally in the implementation to respond to some user request.

Functions and methods should be so designed as to return the maximum amount of information for work that has been already done. The idea here is that the work has been performed already so it would be a waste to automatically discard some of that work. Let the user decide what to discard. Even if the user gets more than they intended to get, they may actually find that extra information useful, and if not, it is no problem for them to discard the extra information. If the disposal happens automatically, the extra amount of work is simply wasted.

According to this principle and since `find` first finds all the matches, it should also return all the matches. It already did the work to find all of them, the resources have been used. Designing the API in such a way that then only one result is returned means to automatically discard the rest of the work, without giving the user a choice to look at this extra information. Again, this could be fixed with #312, but `findall` is already the right kind of implementation which returns all results of the query.

3) Only raise exceptions in exceptional situations.

This is an important principle in C++, because exceptions in C++ cost nothing when they are not triggered, but are very costly when they are triggered. This was a deliberate design trade-off to the indented use case of exceptions, namely to signal situations in which the program cannot continue because of some unrecoverable error.

Python is slow whether exceptions are raised or not ;), so the performance argument is not valid, but it is still a good design to use exceptions only in exceptional cases.

How can you avoid raising exceptions in an API? By using return types that can reflect common "error" states and reserve the exception for the truly exceptional circumstance. In C++, one can return a `std::optional` in those cases where it is not clear whether some query can be fulfilled or not. In Python, we return None instead of some object in a similar situation.

`find` can easily fail, since it is rather common that a query does not return exactly one match. So the right design is to chose a return type that can reflect the fact that there can be zero, one, two, or many results, instead of raising exceptions.

title: Docs for methods `from_..._name(str)`
body:
As far as I can see in the docs, there is no real mention of the methods that create a particle from an exact string match. This can be quite useful if the name of the particle is exactly known and is a better way to create it instead of calling `findall(...)[0]` (as the latter would not give an error if misspelled but could return (a list of) wrong particles).

Also, since the find is deprecated, it's maybe worth to point in the deprecation to these alternatives. Depending on the usecase (if you have the properties of a particle you go for `find{all,iter}`, if you have the exact name, you use `from_evtgen_name` or similar, as the `from_string` is deprecated I think?).

title: Vectorised lookup
body:
Hi there,

Love the project. Just wondering whether there is a way to use something like `from_pdgid` with an iterable of integers. Instantiating a new particle object for each is very expensive, and if I'm just trying to quickly look up a restricted selection of properties en masse it isn't very practical for my use case.

Kind regards,
Jacan.

title: Moving the data files and raw processing to a separate repository
body:
I wanted to ask your opinion about moving the data files including the raw data processing workflows to a separate repository.
We maintain `Corpuscles.jl` (https://github.com/JuliaPhysics/Corpuscles.jl) which is a Julia package for accessing the data files provided by `Particle` and the data update process is manually done.
If we had a separate git repository, we could easily integrate and even automate it.

In KM3NeT we are currently discussing a similar approach for neutrino fluxes and the general acceptance of the idea is pretty good.

title: HTML names: improve compatibility with other XML dialects (e.g. SVG) by only using unicode code-points
body: In [the ntuple wizard](https://gitlab.cern.ch/lhcb-dpa/wp6-analysis-preservation-and-open-data/lhcb-ntuple-wizard-frontend/-/merge_requests/11) I found myself rendering particle HTML names in `viz.js` using some inline SVG. However, the named HTML entities (e.g. `ω`) had to be replaced with code-points (e.g. `ω`) for SVG to understand them. According to [some guy on stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18473312/how-do-i-display-html-encoded-values-in-svg/18473406#18473406) the latter are globally compatible with XML derivatives.

```python
import micropip
```

title: How to extract branching fractions?
body:
Dear Authors,

Thanks for this project. I am looking at a decay file that looks like:

https://gitlab.cern.ch/lhcb-datapkg/Gen/DecFiles/-/blob/master/dkfiles/Bu_JpsiX,ee=JpsiInAcc.dec

and in particular the section:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14037062/161489575-384e4dab-9098-461e-a2b4-7c8c620f4829.png)

Where the numbers are not the absolute fractions, for instance, the  B^+\to J/\psi K^+ process has a BF=1e-3

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14037062/161491166-791323f6-b0b8-4dda-a56c-b8a86fff7ea4.png)

Is there a way to retrieve the actual branching fractions with this project? I could not see anything like that in the documentation, although I might have missed it. Ideally I would do something like:

```python
import pdg_reader as pdgr

ifile=pdgr.load_decays(decay_path)
block=ifile.read_block('B+sig')
#Get PDG fractions instead of the ones in the file (relative?)
l_bf = block.get_bf(kind='absolute')
```

Cheers.

title: Issue publishing
spin of SUSY partners
body:
While checking out the package, I found that some of the SUSY partners of the SM quarks are assigned a spin value of 2 (that in the 2s+1 scheme represents a spin-1/2 particle). For instance, the left-handed partner of up-quark `suL`, which is usually represented by id 1000002, is assigned a value of 2 for its `j_spin`. 
But the squarks are bosons, so their spins are integral and should be 1 or 3.

Am I missing something? Please let me know if this is a known (and intentional) issue.

title: Confusing `is_quarkonium` in Readme
body:
From wikipedia: Quarkonium is a [flavorless](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flavor_(physics)) [meson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meson) whose constituents are a heavy [quark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quark) and its own [antiquark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antiquark) [1].

But lambdas from the readme are:
```
>>> from particle.pdgid import is_meson, three_charge, has_charm, has_bottom, has_top
>>> is_heavy_flavor = lambda x: has_charm(x) or has_bottom(x) or has_top(x)
>>> is_quarkonium = lambda x: is_meson(x) and three_charge(x)==0 and is_heavy_flavor(x)
>>> is_quarkonium(421)
True
```
Do you have a different definition of a quarkonium? since:
```
>>> Particle.from_pdgid(421)
<Particle: name="D0", pdgid=421, mass=1864.84 ± 0.05 MeV>
>>> p=_
>>> p.quarks
'cU'
```

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quarkonium

title: Invalid programmatic_name value for atomic nuclei

When I get the parameters of atomic nuclei whose mass number ends in zero, the programmatic_name attribute has the same format as for elementary particles and ends with "_0":
```python
from particle import Particle
```

```
C1_0
```

title: Possibly a better way to isolate digits of a pdg id?
body:
While trying to efficiently determine charges of a large awkward array of pdg id codes, I wanted to use this library in a numba jit function, but it seems like the lines 902, 903 in the `_digit` function in `particle/src/particle/pdgid/functions.py`:
```
def _digit(pdgid: PDGID_TYPE, loc: int) -> int:
    """
    Provides a convenient index into the PDGID number, whose format is in base 10.

    Returns the digit at position 'loc' given that the right-most digit is at position 1.
    """
    sid = str(abspid(pdgid))
    return int(sid[-loc]) if loc <= len(sid) else 0
```

Are preventing jitting as numba doesnt support direct conversion from `string/char` to `int`, but as even the workaround of conversion from string to bytearray to int is unnecessary overhead, is there a reason why the digits are being isolated in this manner rather than something like:
```
sid = (abspid(pdgid) % 10**loc) // (10 ** (loc - 1))
return nb.int32(sid) #can replace nb with np
```
So far I havent seen any cases where this fails, and it makes atleast the utilities in functions.py (while I had to copy and change the two lines to use the `charge` function with numba) numba friendly

title: Higgs J spin is None
body:
```py
>>> print(particle.Particle.from_pdgid(21).J)
1.0
>>> print(particle.Particle.from_pdgid(22).J)
1.0
>>> print(particle.Particle.from_pdgid(24).J)
1.0
>>> print(particle.Particle.from_pdgid(25).J)
None
```

Shouldn't this return 0 instead of None?

title: No Particle with PDG 20413
body:
### Reproducible

```python
from particle import Particle
Particle.from_pdgid(20413).name

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<python-input-3>", line 1, in <module>
    Particle.from_pdgid(20413).name
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^
  File "/cvmfs/sw-nightlies.hsf.org/key4hep/releases/2025-12-21/x86_64-almalinux9-gcc14.2.0-opt/py-particle/0.24.0-nsy35s/lib/python3.13/site-packages/particle/particle/particle.py", line 983, in from_pdgid
    raise ParticleNotFound(f"Could not find PDGID {value}") from None
particle.particle.particle.ParticleNotFound: Could not find PDGID 20413
```

### Expected result:

According to [this](https://pdg.lbl.gov/2025/reviews/rpp2024-rev-monte-carlo-numbering.pdf) it should be $D_{1}(H)^{+}$
